cards.title: Chest Pain — Diagnostic Algorithm
cards._legacyHtml:
⚠️ Rule out life-threatening causes FIRST
ACS · Aortic dissection · PE · Tension pneumothorax · Tamponade · Oesophageal rupture
1
IMMEDIATE assessment (0–5 min) — Vitals, SpO₂, IV access, 12-lead ECG, troponin. Is this ST-elevation? → STEMI protocol immediately.
2
Character & radiation: Crushing/pressure + radiation jaw/arm = ACS ★ | Tearing/ripping to back = dissection | Pleuritic + haemoptysis = PE | Sharp positional = pericarditis/MSK
3
Risk stratify: HEART score (History, ECG, Age, Risk factors, Troponin). Score 0–3 = low risk. Score ≥7 = high risk → admit + invasive strategy.
4
Serial troponin: High-sensitivity troponin at 0h and 3h (or 0h/1h hs-cTnT protocol). Rise + fall pattern = myocardial injury.
5
Imaging: CXR (widened mediastinum = dissection, pneumothorax, pulmonary oedema). Echo if haemodynamically unstable. CTPA if PE suspected (Wells score ≥2).
DiagnosisKey ECGKey TestImmediate Rx
STEMIST elevation ≥1mm (2+ leads)Troponin (may be normal early)MONA + PCI / thrombolysis
NSTEMIST depression / T-wave inversionTroponin elevatedMONA + anticoagulation + risk stratify
Aortic dissectionNormal or LVHCT aorta with contrast ★Pain control + BP control (target SBP 100–120)
PES1Q3T3 / sinus tachycardiaD-dimer (if low prob) / CTPAAnticoagulation / thrombolysis if massive
PericarditisSaddle ST / PR depressionCRP, EchoNSAIDs + colchicine

cards.title: Breathlessness — Diagnostic Algorithm
cards._legacyHtml:
1
Acute vs chronic? Acute (minutes–hours) = emergency until proven otherwise. Chronic (weeks–months) = systematic workup.
2
Vital signs + SpO₂. SpO₂ <94% = supplemental O₂ now. RR >25 = severe. Unable to speak in full sentences = severe.
3
Respiratory or cardiac? Bilateral crackles + orthopnoea + ↑JVP = cardiac (pulmonary oedema). Wheeze = asthma/COPD. Unilateral reduced breath sounds = pneumothorax/effusion/consolidation.
4
Investigations: CXR + ECG + ABG + FBC + BNP (heart failure marker, >400 pg/ml = HF likely) + peak flow (asthma).
PatternKey FeatureDiagnosis to consider
Wheeze + reversibleDiurnal variation, atopyAsthma
Chronic progressive + smokerBarrel chest, pursed lipsCOPD
Orthopnoea + PND↑JVP, S3, bilateral cracklesHeart failure
Sudden onset + pleuritic painTachycardia, DVT risk factorsPulmonary embolism
Insidious + dry coughBasal crackles, clubbingInterstitial lung disease

cards.title: Jaundice — Diagnostic Algorithm
cards._legacyHtml:
Jaundice = Bilirubin >35 µmol/L (clinically visible)
Scleral jaundice appears first. Dark urine + pale stools = obstructive until proven otherwise.
TypeBilirubinUrineStoolCauses
Pre-hepaticUnconjugated ↑NormalNormal/darkHaemolysis, Gilbert syndrome, neonatal
HepaticBoth ↑DarkVariableHepatitis (viral/alcoholic/drug), cirrhosis, NAFLD
Post-hepaticConjugated ↑Very darkPale/clayGallstones, cholangiocarcinoma, pancreatic head cancer, PSC
1
Pre-hepatic clues Anaemia, pallor, splenomegaly, LDH ↑, haptoglobin ↓, reticulocytes ↑. LFTs: isolated bilirubin ↑ only. No dark urine (unconjugated not filtered).
2
Hepatic clues Hepatomegaly ± tenderness, spider naevi, palmar erythema. ALT ↑↑ (viral) or AST:ALT >2:1 (alcoholic). Synthetic function impaired: PT prolonged, albumin ↓.
3
Obstructive clues Pruritus (bile salts in skin), Courvoisier sign (palpable non-tender GB = pancreatic cancer until proven). ALP and GGT ↑↑ disproportionate to transaminases. USS abdomen first — dilated bile ducts confirm obstruction.
4
Investigations All jaundice: LFTs, FBC, bilirubin fractionation, PT/INR, USS abdomen. Hepatic: HBsAg, anti-HCV, HAV IgM, ANA, AMA. Obstructive: MRCP then ERCP. Refer urgently if: PT prolonged, bilirubin >150, encephalopathy signs.

cards.title: Anaemia — Diagnostic Algorithm
cards._legacyHtml:
1
Step 1 — MCV Classification Low MCV (<80 fL) Microcytic: Iron deficiency, Thalassaemia, Anaemia of chronic disease, Sideroblastic. Normal MCV (80–100) Normocytic: Haemolysis, Acute blood loss, Renal failure (EPO ↓), Bone marrow failure. High MCV (>100) Macrocytic: B12 deficiency, Folate deficiency, Alcohol, Liver disease, Hypothyroidism, Drugs (methotrexate, hydroxyurea).
2
Step 2 — Iron Studies Iron deficiency: ferritin ↓, serum iron ↓, TIBC ↑, transferrin sat ↓. Anaemia of chronic disease: ferritin normal/↑, serum iron ↓, TIBC ↓. Thalassaemia trait: iron studies NORMAL, RBC count ↑ — Mentzer Index (MCV/RBC) <13 = thalassaemia, >13 = IDA.
3
Step 3 — Haemolysis Screen LDH ↑, haptoglobin ↓, unconjugated bilirubin ↑, reticulocytes ↑. Blood film: spherocytes (AIHA/hereditary spherocytosis), schistocytes (TTP/HUS/DIC), target cells (thalassaemia/liver), sickle cells.
4
Red Flags — Urgent Referral New anaemia in elderly (GI cancer), Hb <70 with symptoms, pancytopenia (bone marrow problem), blast cells on film (leukaemia), haemolysis with jaundice, B12 deficiency with neurological features.
🟡 Thalassaemia vs IDA
Both microcytic. KEY: in IDA, RBC count LOW. In thalassaemia trait, RBC count NORMAL or HIGH. Mentzer Index = MCV/RBC: <13 = thalassaemia, >13 = IDA. Confirm with Hb electrophoresis (HbA2 ↑ in beta-thal trait). Common in India (3–4% population). Never give iron to thalassaemia — causes iron overload.

cards.title: Joint Pain — Diagnostic Algorithm
cards._legacyHtml:
1
How many joints? Monoarthritis: septic arthritis ★ (always exclude first — aspirate before treating), gout, pseudogout, reactive arthritis, trauma. Oligoarthritis (2–4): reactive arthritis, psoriatic, IBD-associated. Polyarthritis (5+): RA, SLE, viral (parvovirus B19, hepatitis B), psoriatic, Sjogren's.
2
Inflammatory vs Mechanical Inflammatory: morning stiffness >30 min, worse at rest, improves with movement, systemic features, elevated ESR/CRP. Mechanical: worse with activity, improves at rest, stiffness <30 min, no systemic features, normal inflammatory markers.
3
Pattern of joint involvement Symmetric small joints (MCPs, PIPs, wrists): RA. DIP joints: OA, psoriatic. 1st MTP: gout. Large joints: OA, reactive. Axial spine + SI joints: ankylosing spondylitis (young male, HLA-B27, morning back stiffness).
4
Key investigations All inflammatory: FBC, ESR, CRP, RF, anti-CCP, ANA, uric acid, X-rays. Suspected septic: blood cultures + JOINT ASPIRATION (URGENT). Suspected reactive: swabs, HLA-B27. Suspected SLE: ANA, anti-dsDNA, complement C3/C4 ↓.
🟡 Septic Arthritis — Never Miss ★
Any hot swollen single joint + fever = septic arthritis until aspirated and proven otherwise. Aspirate FIRST — even if gout suspected (they can co-exist). WBC >50,000/mm3 in synovial fluid = septic. Staph aureus = most common organism. IV antibiotics + washout often needed. Delays cause permanent joint destruction within hours.

cards.title: Oedema & FUO — Diagnostic Algorithms
cards._legacyHtml:
OEDEMA ALGORITHM
TypeDistributionPittingKey CausesInvestigations
CardiacBilateral ankles, sacral (bedridden)YesHeart failure, constrictive pericarditisBNP, Echo, CXR
RenalPeriorbital (morning ★), generalisedYesNephrotic syndrome, AKI, CKDUrinalysis (protein ★), albumin, creatinine
HepaticAscites + peripheralYesCirrhosis, portal hypertensionLFTs, albumin, USS
LymphoedemaUnilateral limb, non-pittingNo — firm, fibroticPost-surgical, filarial, malignancyUSS (exclude DVT), lymphoscintigraphy
DVT ★Unilateral leg, warm, erythematousVariableImmobility, surgery, cancer, OCPD-dimer + USS Doppler URGENTLY
Drug-inducedBilateral anklesYesAmlodipine ★, NSAIDs, steroids, glitazonesMedication review — often resolves on withdrawal
FEVER OF UNKNOWN ORIGIN (FUO)
FUO Definition
Temperature >38.3°C, duration >3 weeks, no diagnosis after 1 week thorough investigation.
Category%Common Causes in India
Infection ★35%TB (most common ★), IE, abscesses, brucellosis, typhoid, malaria, HIV
Malignancy25%Lymphoma ★, leukaemia, renal cell carcinoma, HCC
Non-infectious inflammatory20%Adult Still's disease (fever+rash+arthritis), SLE, vasculitis, sarcoidosis, GCA
Drug fever10%Antibiotics, anticonvulsants — eosinophilia clue
🟡 FUO in India — Think TB First
TB is the leading infectious cause of FUO in India — extrapulmonary TB (lymph node, mesenteric, miliary) presents with FUO and often normal CXR. Investigations: IGRA (QuantiFERON-TB Gold), CT chest (miliary pattern), lymph node biopsy, bone marrow biopsy if pancytopenia, CBNAAT on tissue for rapid molecular diagnosis.

cards.title: Back Pain — Red Flags & Differential
cards._legacyHtml:
Back Pain Red Flags — TUNA FISH ★
Trauma · Urinary/bowel symptoms (cauda equina ★) · Neurological deficit · Age >50 new onset · Fever/systemic illness · IV drug use · Steroids/immunosuppression · History of cancer
DiagnosisKey FeaturesInvestigation
Mechanical LBP (90%)Worse activity, better rest, age 20–55, no neurologyNone needed initially — X-ray if >6 weeks
Disc prolapse + radiculopathyUnilateral leg pain below knee, positive SLR, dermatomalMRI lumbar spine if >6 weeks or neurology
Cauda equina ★ EMERGENCYBilateral leg weakness, saddle anaesthesia, urinary retention/incontinenceEMERGENCY MRI — surgery within 6 hours
Spinal stenosisBilateral leg pain worse walking, relieved sitting (neurogenic claudication), elderlyMRI lumbar spine
Ankylosing spondylitisAge <40, insidious, morning stiffness >1hr, improves exercise, SI joint tendernessMRI SI joints, HLA-B27, ESR/CRP
Vertebral fractureSudden onset minimal trauma, elderly, known cancer, steroid useX-ray, MRI (acute vs chronic)
Spinal infectionFever, night sweats, IV drug use, immunocompromised, relentless painMRI spine urgent, blood cultures, CRP ↑↑↑
MalignancyAge >50, known cancer, night pain, weight loss, does not resolve with restMRI whole spine, bone scan, CT CAP

cards.title: Headache — Diagnostic Algorithm
cards._legacyHtml:
🚨 RED FLAG headaches — exclude immediately
Thunderclap (worst headache of life) · Fever + neck stiffness · Neurological deficit · New headache >50yrs · Headache waking from sleep · Progressive worsening · Post-trauma
1
Thunderclap headache? Maximal intensity within seconds → SAH until proven otherwise. CT head immediately (98% sensitivity <6h). If CT negative → LP for xanthochromia at 12 hours.
2
Fever + neck stiffness? → Meningism. Do not wait for LP if meningococcal suspected — give ceftriaxone 2g IV immediately then CT/LP.
3
Classify primary headache: Migraine (unilateral, throbbing, nausea, photophobia, aura) | TTH (bilateral, pressure, mild–moderate) | Cluster (unilateral periorbital, autonomic features, restlessness).
4
Migraine acute Rx: Sumatriptan 50–100mg + NSAID (ibuprofen 400mg) + antiemetic. Prophylaxis if >4/month: propranolol/topiramate/amitriptyline.
TypeLocationDurationKey features
MigraineUnilateral (60%)4–72hNausea, photophobia, phonophobia, aggravated by activity
Tension-typeBilateral, band30min–7daysPressing, mild–moderate, not aggravated by activity
ClusterUnilateral periorbital15–180 min, 1–8/dayLacrimation, rhinorrhoea, ptosis, restlessness
SAHGlobalSudden, persistentThunderclap, worst ever, neck stiffness

cards.title: Syncope — Diagnostic Algorithm
cards._legacyHtml:
1
Confirm syncope vs other LOC. True syncope = transient, self-limiting, complete recovery. Rule out: seizure (post-ictal confusion, tongue-bite), hypoglycaemia (check BG), TIA (no LOC in TIA).
2
ECG immediately. Arrhythmia? Long QT? WPW? Complete heart block? → Cardiac syncope = high mortality risk.
3
Classify: Reflex/vasovagal (prodrome: nausea, diaphoresis) | Orthostatic (postural BP drop ≥20mmHg within 3 min of standing) | Cardiac (exertional, no prodrome)
4
San Francisco Syncope Rule (high-risk): HF history · Haematocrit <30% · Abnormal ECG · Shortness of breath · SBP <90 on arrival. Any ONE = admit.

cards.title: Acute Abdomen — Differential Algorithm
cards._legacyHtml:
Peritonism signs = surgical emergency
Rigid abdomen + rebound tenderness + guarding → do NOT delay surgical review
LocationCausesKey SignsTest
RUQCholecystitis ★, choledocholithiasis, hepatitisMurphy's sign, jaundice, RUQ tendernessUSS, LFTs, bilirubin
RIFAppendicitis ★, ovarian pathology (F), ectopic (F)Rovsing's, McBurney's, psoas signUSS/CT, β-hCG, FBC
LIFDiverticulitis ★, sigmoid volvulusLIF tenderness, bowel habit changeCT abdomen/pelvis
EpigastricPUD ★, pancreatitis, GORD, inferior MIEpigastric guarding, back radiationAmylase/lipase, ECG
GeneralisedPerforation, mesenteric ischaemia, DKABoard-like rigidity, absent bowel soundsCT + erect CXR, lactate
🟡 Alvarado score for appendicitis
Migration to RIF (1) + Anorexia (1) + Nausea (1) + Tenderness RIF (2) + Rebound (1) + Elevated temp (1) + Leukocytosis (2) + Shift left (1) = /10. Score ≥7 = high probability → surgery. Score 4–6 = observe + USS. Score ≤3 = unlikely appendicitis.
